text: Three kinematic variables (pelvis vertical oscillation during ground contact normalized to height, minimum knee joint angle during ground contact, and minimum horizontal pelvis velocity) explain 39% of the variability in running economy (locomotory energy cost).
population: endurance runners of mixed competitive standards

text: Four kinematic variables (minimum horizontal pelvis velocity, shank touchdown angle, duty factor, and trunk forward lean) explain 31% of the variability in running performance.
population: endurance runners of mixed competitive standards

text: Minimizing vertical oscillation of the pelvis during ground contact improves running economy.
population: endurance runners of mixed competitive standards

text: Maintaining higher minimum horizontal pelvis velocity (less braking) during ground contact is associated with both better running economy and faster performance times.
population: endurance runners of mixed competitive standards

text: Greater knee flexion (lower minimum knee joint angle) during ground contact is associated with poorer running economy.
population: endurance runners of mixed competitive standards

text: Shank touchdown angle, duty factor, and trunk forward lean are key technique variables that predict running performance independently of running economy.
population: endurance runners of mixed competitive standards

text: Running technique as a whole explains a substantial proportion of the variance in both running economy and performance, making it a trainable target for improvement alongside physiological variables.
population: endurance runners of mixed competitive standards
